one_line_takeaway: GF-Score decomposes the certified GREAT robustness score per class and adds four welfare-economics-style disparity metrics plus an attack-free calibration step, showing across 22 RobustBench models that aggregate certified robustness can mask severe per-class gaps (e.g., 'cat' is worst in 76% of CIFAR-10 models, two ImageNet models have zero certified robustness on at least one class) and that more-robust models tend to be less fair across classes.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10, uncalibrated GF-Score achieves Spearman rho=0.662 with RobustBench rankings (matching the original GREAT paper's reported 0.6618); the attack-free self-calibration (T*=2.70) raises this to rho=0.871, versus the original paper's attack-based (C&W) calibrated rho=0.897. On ImageNet, uncalibrated rho=0.900 (vs. the original paper's reported 0.800 uncalibrated, no calibration was performed in the original paper for ImageNet), and GF-Score's attack-free calibration (T*=0.10) reaches rho=1.000 (perfect rank agreement). The class-conditional decomposition is exactly consistent (zero numerical error) across all 22 models. Class 'cat' is the most vulnerable CIFAR-10 class in 13/17 models (76%), 'automobile' is the most robust in 10/17 (59%); on ImageNet, class n04525038 (viaduct) is worst in 3/5 models and n12057211 (cattail) is best in all 5. RDI on CIFAR-10 ranges from 0.11 (Wu2020, lowest disparity) to 0.435 (Augustin2020, highest disparity, Table 3); on ImageNet RDI ranges roughly 1.13-1.23 (much higher, attributed to 1,000-class diversity), and two ImageNet models (Salman_R18, Wong2020) have WCR=0.000, meaning certified robustness is zero for at least one class despite positive aggregate scores. A positive correlation is observed between aggregate GREAT Score and RDI on both datasets (more-robust models show more cross-class disparity), and FP-GREAT re-ranking with lambda=0.5 shifts model rankings substantially versus the aggregate score (e.g., Augustin2020 drops from 2nd to 5th; Wu2020 rises from 16th to 14th, per Table 3/Appendix).
core_contribution: The paper introduces GF-Score (GREAT-Fairness Score), a post-hoc auditing framework built on top of the existing certified GREAT Score (Li et al., 2024) for adversarial robustness. Its own contributions are three: (1) a class-conditional decomposition of the GREAT Score into per-class certified robustness values, proven to sum exactly (with zero numerical error) to the original aggregate score, plus Hoeffding-inequality concentration bounds (Propositions 1 and 2) on the per-class estimates and on the Robustness Disparity Index (RDI); (2) four disparity metrics adapted from welfare economics/fairness theory to quantify how certified robustness is spread across classes: Robustness Disparity Index (RDI, max-min range), Normalized Robustness Gini Coefficient (NRGC), Worst-Case Class Robustness (WCR, a Rawlsian maximin score), and Fairness-Penalized GREAT Score (FP-GREAT = mean score minus lambda*RDI, analogous to the UNDP's Inequality-Adjusted HDI); (3) an attack-free self-calibration procedure that tunes the GREAT Score's softmax/sigmoid temperature parameter by maximizing Spearman rank correlation with publicly available clean accuracies (a two-phase coarse-then-fine grid search over T in [0.01, 10.0]), replacing the original method's reliance on the C&W attack for calibration, and a secondary ranking-stability calibration variant. The framework itself does not modify training or propose a new attack/defense; it is a diagnostic/auditing layer applied to already-trained, already-published robust models.
threat_model: The paper does not introduce a new threat model. It inherits the standard certified-robustness threat model of the underlying GREAT Score: an adversary perturbs an input within an l2 ball (for the 17 CIFAR-10 models evaluated) or l-infinity ball (for the 5 ImageNet models evaluated) to flip a classifier's prediction, and the GREAT Score's local score g(x) provides a certified lower bound on the l2 perturbation magnitude needed to cause misclassification, computed via forward passes on generative-model samples (no adversarial attack is executed). GF-Score's novelty is not in this threat model but in decomposing the resulting certified bound by class and quantifying cross-class disparity in the guarantee -- i.e., the 'threat' being audited is that a model's certified protection may be very unevenly distributed across classes even when the aggregate certified bound looks strong.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the framework inherits GREAT Score's dependence on the generative model G approximating the true data distribution well enough for the certified bound to be meaningful; (2) the certified local score relies on the softmax/sigmoid confidence margin, which can be loose for models with poorly calibrated outputs; (3) the self-calibration procedure assumes a monotonic relationship between adversarial robustness and clean accuracy, which the authors say holds within a defense family but may not hold across fundamentally different architectures; (4) the ImageNet evaluation covers only 5 models due to computational constraints on extracting logits for 50K images over 1,000 classes, so the authors note scaling to more models would strengthen the findings; (5) concentration bounds are provided only for RDI, not for NRGC or WCR, which the authors flag as open theoretical questions. Not stated by authors but evident from the text: all findings are correlational/observational (no causal claim about why 'cat' is hardest, though authors speculate it reflects intrinsic visual similarity to 'dog' rather than training artifacts); the framework audits certified bounds already computed by GREAT Score and does not itself provide any mechanism to fix or mitigate the observed disparity -- it is diagnostic only.

datasets_used: GAN-generated samples used for GREAT Score computation, per the original GREAT Score protocol
quantitative_results: CIFAR-10 uncalibrated Spearman rho with RobustBench rankings = 0.662
Class 'cat' is worst CIFAR-10 class in 13/17 models (76%); 'automobile' best in 10/17 (59%)
ImageNet RDI range: approximately 1.13 to 1.23 across the 5 models
Two ImageNet models (Salman_R18, Wong2020) have WCR = 0.000 (zero certified robustness on at least one class)
Class-conditional decomposition sums to the aggregate GREAT Score exactly (zero numerical error) across all 22 models
FP-GREAT (lambda=0.5) re-ranking: Augustin2020 drops from rank 2 to rank 5; Wu2020 rises from rank 16 to rank 14 on CIFAR-10
Interpretive bound example: with n_k=1,000 samples/class, K=10, delta=0.05, the per-class concentration bound gives |Omega_hat_k - Omega_k| <= 0.069 for all classes simultaneously
baselines_compared: Original GREAT Score (Li et al., 2024) -- aggregate-only certified robustness metric; its attack-based (C&W) calibration achieved rho=0.897 on CIFAR-10 and no calibration was reported for ImageNet (rho=0.800 uncalibrated)
RobustBench (Croce et al., 2021) robust-accuracy rankings, used as the ground-truth ranking target for both uncalibrated and calibrated Spearman correlation comparisons

Table 1 qualitatively compares GF-Score against AutoAttack, RobustBench, randomized smoothing (Cohen et al., 2019), GREAT Score, and training-time fairness methods FRL, CFA, WAT, BAT, FAAL, CODA, CSR on the dimensions: certified, per-class, attack-free, post-hoc, fairness metrics -- GF-Score is presented as the only method satisfying all five
